does: This paper introduces Relevance Patching (RelP), a method for identifying which components in language models cause specific behaviors. RelP combines activation patching, the gold-standard interpretability technique, with Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP), a neural network attribution method. The core innovation replaces gradient-based coefficients in attribution patching with LRP's propagation coefficients, which backward-propagate output relevance through network layers while conserving relevance and improving signal-to-noise ratios. The method requires only two forward passes and one backward pass, maintaining the computational efficiency of attribution patching while substantially improving its faithfulness. The authors validate RelP across multiple language models and tasks, demonstrating it more accurately approximates ground-truth activation patching results than standard attribution patching approaches.
connects: This work extends a line of research in mechanistic interpretability focused on localizing causal components within neural networks. Activation patching, established as the standard method for this task, is prohibitively expensive at scale. Attribution patching emerged as a faster gradient-based approximation but introduced noise and reliability issues in deep nonlinear models. RelP connects to the broader interpretability literature by leveraging Layer-wise Relevance Propagation, an attribution technique developed to improve signal quality in complex networks through principled relevance redistribution. The paper bridges mechanistic interpretability—which aims to understand how individual network components implement functions—with classical neural network attribution methods, combining their respective strengths to address a persistent efficiency-faithfulness tradeoff in circuit discovery.
enables: RelP enables scalable circuit discovery in large language models by providing a faithful approximation that scales better than activation patching while maintaining higher reliability than basic attribution methods. This unlocks the ability to interpret increasingly large models across diverse tasks and behaviors at lower computational cost. The work points toward improved mechanistic interpretability pipelines that can handle industrial-scale models, potentially supporting safety analysis and behavior verification. Future research could extend RelP to other architectures like transformers with varying depths, investigate how propagation rules from LRP transfer across model families, or combine RelP with causal discovery methods to construct more complete circuit diagrams. The technique may also enable real-time interpretation tools and automated circuit discovery frameworks for auditing model behaviors before deployment.
